text: The surgeon visually evaluates the rectal prolapse, assessing its length and size, while examining the functional status of the anal sphincter complex.
The surgeon performs a submucosal infiltration of adrenaline to minimize bleeding during the dissection of the rectal prolapse.
The surgeon performs a careful circular incision of the rectal wall using a monopolar diathermy, effectively isolating the rectum from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon ensures the mesorectum remains visible to partially fill the pre-sacral space during the rectal prolapse procedure.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection of the rectum, taking particular care to avoid damaging the posterior wall of the vagina, which is visible in close proximity to the surgical field.
The posterior wall of the vagina is clearly visible as the surgeon opens the Douglas pouch, providing a view of the anatomical structures in this area.
Following the isolation and detachment of the rectum, the surgeon applies gentle traction to the proximal colon to evaluate the extent of the redundant bowel that needs to be excised.
The surgeon uses a sealing instrument to perform a rapid dissection around the pericolonic tissue, facilitating the separation of the colon from surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs a posterior pelvic floor repair using non-absorbable stitches to reinforce the filter complex, with the mesorectum visible in the pre-sacral space.
The surgeon resects the redundant segment of the colon and performs an end-to-end coloanal anastomosis using interrupted non-absorbable stitches.
The surgeon performs a final check to assess the integrity of the coloanal anastomosis, visually inspecting the area for any gaps and adding additional stitches as needed.
